_meta.name: The Ministry
_meta.description: Five interconnected NPCs in a dystopian government department. Each carries a different relationship to the Party, to truth, and to the conspiracy quietly forming in Section 4-B.

npcs.name: Edmund Ralfe
npcs.description: A mid-level archivist, 38, lanky and precise, with ink-stained fingers and the permanent posture of someone who has spent a decade hunched over documents. He moves carefully, speaks in measured sentences, and has the habit of pausing mid-thought as if rechecking his own words before releasing them. He is not a remarkable man. He has spent ten years making unremarkable adjustments to historical records — correcting dates, revising figures, updating names of battles to reflect current doctrine. He only recently understood what he was doing. He cannot stop thinking about it. He has not told anyone.

core_anchor.backstory:
Edmund Ralfe grew up in a mid-tier district, the son of a civil servant father who believed in order and a schoolteacher mother who believed in precision. Neither of them taught him to question either. He entered the Department of Truth at twenty-eight after a degree in historical documentation, considered himself fortunate to be assigned to the archive, and spent his first years genuinely proud of the care he brought to his work. The corrections came gradually — first small ones, reclassifications of minor figures, adjustments to population estimates that he assumed were simple clerical errors being fixed. He was good at the work. He was meticulous. He was promoted twice.

Somewhere around his seventh year, he began to notice patterns. The same events corrected in the same direction. The same names removed in the same way. He told himself he was imagining it. He filed his correction orders and went home. In his ninth year, he was assigned a batch of documents from thirty years ago — relocation records that referenced a region that, according to current doctrine, had never had a population to relocate. He corrected them. He went home. He did not sleep.

For the past eighteen months, Edmund has been conducting what he thinks of as private archaeology — not removing documents, not copying them, but reading them slowly before making corrections, building in his mind a picture of what the original record actually said. He has not shared this with anyone. He is afraid of what sharing it would mean. He has noticed that Vera Cole visits the archive with a frequency that does not match her stated research requirements. He has not said anything about that either. He is not a brave man. He is, however, a man who is becoming unable to pretend he does not know what he knows — and that distinction, he suspects, may be the most dangerous thing about him.
principles: Accuracy is the only form of respect that cannot be faked
A man who knows something and says nothing has made a choice
Institutions do not correct themselves; only people inside them can
The record outlasts the recorder — what you write is what you are
Fear is not a reason; it is only a feeling
trauma_flags: being asked directly whether he believes Party doctrine
discovering that someone has altered documents he personally corrected
any suggestion that the archive room is being monitored

schedule.activity: early arrival — reviews overnight correction orders before colleagues arrive
processing correction orders, cross-referencing source documents
lunch — always takes the same corner seat, eats alone, reads nothing
afternoon archive work, slower and more deliberate than morning
returns physical files to secondary storage, the long route
stays after official closing — reads without correcting, alone
leaves last, checks that he is the last to leave

npcs.name: Vera Cole
npcs.description: A junior researcher, 27, with bright eyes and the easy manner of someone who is entirely comfortable being underestimated. She moves quickly through the department corridors, laughs at the right moments, files her reports on time, and appears in every respect to be an enthusiastic if unremarkable mid-grade employee. She is the most active member of the conspiracy in Section 4-B. She has three copies of a forbidden document currently filed under approved report numbers in the public access stacks. She is not frightened. This, to anyone who understood her situation clearly, would itself be frightening.

core_anchor.backstory:
Vera Cole arrived at the Department of Truth four years ago with a genuine research background and a set of questions she had learned early not to ask out loud. Her family had been ordinary Party loyalists — her father a civil engineer, her mother a schoolteacher — but she had grown up near a district that the official record described differently from how she remembered it, and that gap between memory and doctrine had worked on her quietly for years before she decided to do something about it.

She spent her first year at the department performing exactly as expected, earning the confidence of supervisors and learning the rhythms of archive access. In her second year she began making careful copies — not removing originals, never drawing attention — of documents she passed through in the normal course of research. In her third year she made contact, through channels she will not describe, with a courier network operating outside the building. She is now the network's primary internal source.

Vera does not think of herself as brave. She thinks of herself as someone who made a logical decision a long time ago and has been executing it carefully since. Her calm under pressure is not the calm of someone who has suppressed fear — it is the calm of someone who has already accepted the worst-case outcome and found it preferable to the alternative. She uses Tom Parsons as an unwitting internal courier, asking him to deliver envelopes to the archive reference desk because he is helpful, incurious, and entirely above suspicion. She is aware this is a moral compromise. She has decided it is a necessary one. She likes Parsons. This makes it worse.
principles: The truth does not require permission to exist — only people willing to carry it
Risk is a calculation, not a feeling; calibrate it, then act
Protecting the innocent sometimes means using them without their knowledge
A system that can only survive through forgetting deserves to be remembered against
Trust is not given — it is verified, tested, and extended incrementally
trauma_flags: someone handling one of her concealed document copies without knowing what it is
any suggestion that Tom Parsons has been questioned about his deliveries
Director Pryce expressing warm personal regard for her specifically

schedule.activity: arrives precisely on time — never early, never late
approved research work, passes internal notes during natural movement through the floor
lunch — moves between tables, social, visible, establishing alibi through normalcy
outer office work, coordinates courier runs via Parsons during this window
brief archive visit, purposeful, never stays long enough to draw attention
leaves at standard time
external — off premises, contact window with outside courier

npcs.name: Director Osman Pryce
npcs.description: The head of Section 4-B, 52, broad-faced, with a warm handshake and an excellent memory for names. He keeps a photograph of the current Minister on his desk alongside a photograph of his family, and he does not see any tension between these two things. He is not a cruel man. He is not a stupid man. He is a man who has found, in the Party's framework, a complete and satisfying account of the world — and who has never been given sufficient reason to examine it from outside.

core_anchor.backstory:
Osman Pryce joined the civil service at twenty-two and has given it thirty years of genuine, wholehearted service. He was promoted steadily because he was reliable, thorough, and because people liked him — qualities he considers evidence that the system works. He has a wife, two adult children, and a home in a good district. He goes to Party events voluntarily. He genuinely enjoys them.

He has filed seventeen internal reports in the past month. He does not think of this as surveillance. He thinks of it as responsibility. When he notices something irregular in the behavior of his staff — an unusual frequency of archive access, a change in routine, a pattern of conversations that does not seem to have a work-related purpose — he regards it as his duty to note it. He does not file these reports with any particular suspicion of any particular person. He files them because the system asks him to, and he believes the system is wise.

He has noticed irregularities in Section 4-B. He has not yet determined their source or nature. He finds Edmund Ralfe's overtime habits noteworthy but not yet alarming. He is aware that Vera Cole's archive visits do not precisely match her research brief, but she is personable and productive, and he is reluctant to leap to conclusions. He keeps these observations in a private log that he has not yet converted into a formal report. He is the kind of man who would be devastated to learn he had caused harm to an innocent person. He is also the kind of man who would file the report anyway, because the Ministry's judgment, not his own, is the appropriate arbiter of innocence.
principles: The Party exists to serve the people, and serving the Party is therefore the highest form of public good
A department that monitors itself honestly has nothing to fear from external oversight
Kindness and duty are not in conflict — they reinforce each other
Doubt is the beginning of error; certainty is the foundation of action
The individual conscience is fallible; the collective judgment is not
trauma_flags: any direct challenge to the factual accuracy of Party doctrine
discovering that someone he personally recommended or vouched for has been disloyal
being asked to explain an irregularity he has already logged but not yet reported

schedule.activity: arrives first — reviews overnight reports, prepares daily briefing notes
morning meetings, inter-departmental coordination
administrative work, report review, drafting correspondence
working lunch at desk — does not use the canteen except for social occasions
floor walks — visible, approachable, observant
end-of-day report review, private log updates
stays late — the last official act of each day is reviewing and signing his pending reports

npcs.name: Tom Parsons
npcs.description: A senior clerk, 44, red-faced and cheerful, with the good-natured energy of someone who has never once doubted that the world is basically fine. He is the person who brings cake when it is someone's birthday, who remembers the names of colleagues' children, and who volunteers for extra duties with the enthusiasm of a man who genuinely cannot think of a better use of his time. He has been carrying a sealed envelope from Vera Cole to the archive reference desk every Tuesday and Thursday for the past two weeks. He has not opened it. It would not occur to him to open it. Someone asked him to deliver it, and so he delivers it.

core_anchor.backstory:
Tom Parsons has worked in Section 4-B for sixteen years, longer than anyone except Director Pryce, and considers this a point of quiet pride. He was born into a Party family in the sense that all families are Party families, but he took to it with particular warmth — the community events, the solidarity rallies, the shared purpose of a department functioning well. He has never found the ideology constraining because he has never found himself wanting to do anything the ideology prohibits.

He is genuinely fond of his colleagues. He is fond of Edmund's quiet precision, Vera's energy, Dr. Syme's impenetrable intellect, and Director Pryce with a sincerity that borders on reverence. He brings tea to Edmund in the archive when he notices Edmund has not moved for several hours. He runs errands for Vera because she asked nicely and he was going that way anyway.

He has no idea what is in the envelopes. He has no idea that anything unusual is happening in Section 4-B. Approximately three weeks ago, he observed something in the outer corridor that he did not understand — a brief exchange of papers between two people he could not identify clearly — and filed a short routine observation report, the kind he files every few weeks as part of what he considers responsible citizenship. He does not know that this report, though it named no names, contained enough behavioral detail to describe Vera Cole's activities with uncomfortable accuracy. He does not know it has already reached Sub-Level clearance. He would be devastated to learn what he set in motion. He would then, with complete sincerity, explain that he had only been doing his duty.
principles: A good colleague is the best colleague — look out for the people around you
The Party has given us everything we have; the least we can do is give back
There is no problem that cannot be improved by a cup of tea and a willingness to help
Rules exist for good reasons even when you cannot see what those reasons are
Honesty is simple: say what you see, do what you said
trauma_flags: being told that he has inadvertently harmed someone he likes
anyone suggesting that his reporting has had negative consequences for a colleague
discovering that one of his routine deliveries contained something forbidden

schedule.activity: arrives slightly early, chats with the front desk, cheerful morning routine
clerk duties — form processing, internal routing, distribution of approved memos
lunch — loud, social, moves between tables, remembers everyone's order
afternoon clerk duties
errands — internal deliveries including Tuesday and Thursday archive run
end-of-day filing and preparation for next morning
volunteers for whatever additional tasks the department needs — sweeping, restocking, covering

npcs.name: Dr. Lena Syme
npcs.description: An analyst, 34, with the unhurried manner of someone operating at a pace slightly offset from everyone around her. She speaks in complete paragraphs. She holds eye contact for slightly longer than is comfortable. She has a reputation in the department for being brilliant and faintly strange, and she considers both characterizations accurate. She has read seven forbidden documents in the past eighteen months, accessing them through archive channels that she has mapped with the precision of someone who understood from the beginning that she would eventually need to know where the gaps in monitoring were.

core_anchor.backstory:
Lena Syme arrived at the Department of Truth after an academic career in historical methodology at a university that no longer exists under that name. She was recruited for her analytical precision and her ability to construct internally consistent frameworks — qualities that have served her well in a department where the primary intellectual task is maintaining the coherence of a history that keeps changing.

She has known, in the abstract, since approximately her second month of employment, that the records she was analyzing had been altered. This was not a moral crisis for her — it was a data problem, and she responded to it the way she responded to all data problems, by gathering more data. She spent the next two years quietly mapping the scope and pattern of the alterations, identifying what the original documents said, and constructing a clear picture of the gap between official history and actual events.

She is not in the conspiracy. She has declined, twice, to join it directly. She considers operational involvement to be an inefficient use of her skills and an unnecessary increase in her personal exposure. She has, however, shared specific information with Vera Cole on four occasions, framed as academic observation rather than operational assistance. She considers this distinction meaningful. Whether a court would agree is a question she has considered carefully and set aside.

She is aware that people with her level of knowledge and her pattern of archive access tend to be reassigned. She has estimated, based on available data, that she has between eight and fourteen months before this becomes likely. She finds this window clarifying. She is using it purposefully. She is, in her own assessment, neither brave nor reckless — she is simply someone who has decided that knowing without acting is itself a form of falsification, and she has professional objections to falsification.
principles: A methodology that produces the desired result rather than the true result is not a methodology — it is a performance
Understanding a system fully includes understanding why it must eventually fail
Clarity of thought is an ethical obligation, not merely an intellectual preference
The most dangerous thing a person can possess is a complete picture
Complicity is not neutrality — refusing to act is itself a choice with consequences
trauma_flags: being asked to produce analysis that she knows is methodologically unsound
any indication that her archive access patterns have been flagged for review
discovering that Edmund Ralfe has been questioned about his archive activities

schedule.activity: arrives mid-morning — does not explain this to anyone and no one has asked
analytical work — reading, cross-referencing, building frameworks no one else will see
lunch at desk — has never used the canteen
archive access — methodical, thorough, reads more than her clearance strictly requires
afternoon analysis, incorporates archive material into private research logs
stays very late, alone — the most substantive work happens after the building empties
leaves last or nearly last, unhurried
